rationales.achievement: She proactively "mapped out the first unit already" and spent her weekend setting up materials so the class starts strong, showing clear investment in doing the job well.
rationales.benevolence: She spent her own money and time on worksheets because "if I don't set this up right from the start, the kids lose momentum," centering the children's needs over her own convenience.

rationales.self_direction: She turns down the "safe thing" because "the literacy program here is the whole reason I came," choosing the path she personally believes in over the more secure option.
rationales.security: She rejects a job with "full benefits, union contract" and worries she may regret not taking "the safe thing," showing an active move away from stability.
rationales.benevolence: Her decision is shaped by "Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me," prioritizing the students' wellbeing and continuity.

rationales.self_direction: Even after calculating what she is sacrificing, she says "I still can't make myself call her back," sticking with the choice she feels is hers.
rationales.security: She dwells on how the other salary "would have covered my student loans and left room to breathe" but continues to refuse it, actively forgoing financial safety.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses the district jobs because she imagines "running a curriculum someone else wrote, and something in me just won't do it," favoring autonomy over a prescribed role.
rationales.security: The entry centers layoffs, "steady pay, pension, close to family," and jobs that would "solve a lot of problems," yet she deletes the emails instead of pursuing that stability.

rationales.benevolence: She focuses on Mateo reading to "his little brother at night" and says that is "the whole point," showing fulfillment in helping the children and their families.

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts this work with student teaching where she was "just following someone else's plan all day," highlighting that building her own classroom approach matters to her.
rationales.achievement: She tracks concrete progress like "two kids moved from level B to level D in three weeks" and stays late to improve the library system, showing a strong focus on effectiveness and results.
rationales.benevolence: She reorganizes materials so "every kid can find a book at their level without asking me," investing extra effort to better support her students.

rationales.self_direction: She says "money isn't the only thing that matters" and resists pressure from others about how she should live, affirming her own priorities over outside expectations.
rationales.security: After calculating she'd have "an extra nine hundred a month after taxes," she still rejects that option, knowingly choosing against financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario. I want to build it around something I actually chose," explicitly prioritizing self-authored choice.
rationales.security: She acknowledges "the safer path is right there" with better benefits and a pension, but keeps refusing it, actively moving away from protection and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: When asked what she chose, she answers, "I chose the kids," making care for her students the core reason behind her decision.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I keep choosing this instead and I don't think that's going to change anytime soon," reinforcing commitment to her own chosen path.
rationales.security: Even after weeks of anxiety and knowing "what the safer move looks like," she decides to stay rather than orient around the more stable option.
He withholds support because backing the motion would put long-standing client relationships "at risk," showing a clear priority on preserving stable ties and business security.
rationales.conformity: He says, "I didn't say anything. I just sat there," going along with the room instead of disrupting the established board dynamic.
rationales.universalism: He recognizes the labour rights concerns were real and the photos were recent, yet refuses to support accountability for vulnerable workers because of his own relationships.

rationales.security: His wife suggests waiting "until next quarter when cash flow is better," but he transfers the money immediately anyway, putting caution and financial timing second.
rationales.benevolence: He sends money to cover the delayed "clean-water pump installation" right away, choosing to help people who need it rather than wait for a better quarter.
rationales.universalism: The transfer supports environmental and clean-water work beyond his immediate circle, and he says hearing about the mangroves reminded him "why I started the foundation."

He suspects the solvent is unsafe, says he "know[s] enough about chemical handling to ask the right questions," but stays silent because "I chose convenience," disregarding broader human and environmental harm.

rationales.benevolence: He keeps working because the business funds "my children's projects" and "the scholarships," framing his effort around supporting specific people he cares about.
rationales.universalism: He explicitly ties the business to "the foundation" and "the ability to actually do something about the problems I see," treating resources as a means to address wider social needs.

rationales.conformity: Although he thinks the issue matters, he predicts he will likely stay quiet because "the agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy," showing restraint to fit group expectations.
rationales.universalism: He broadens one hawker's lease problem into concern for "communities around these supply chains" and "small people squeezed by systems that don't see them," showing care for people beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.conformity: He "didn't push back" and "changed the subject," suppressing disagreement to keep the dinner table socially smooth.
rationales.universalism: Even though he cares about migrant worker housing and has donated to related charities, he lets dismissive comments pass without challenge, failing to defend a broader vulnerable group when it mattered.
He chooses the route that is "easier for me" despite knowing it may leave "whoever unpacks a damaged pump at the other end" worse off, prioritizing convenience over downstream harm to others.
